mission: The Moot Board of Governors (MBG) unites the pre-law societies of universities across Canada under a single national body. Chaired by the presidents of its member societies, the Board coordinates national moot competitions, shares advocacy resources, and builds a community of aspiring legal advocates from coast to coast.
advocacy: We organize and sanction national moot competitions, setting common standards so that students from any member society can compete on a level playing field.
community: We connect pre-law society leaders across provinces, creating a network for collaboration, shared events, and mentorship between schools.
education: We pool resources, moot problems, and training materials so every member society can prepare its students for law school and legal careers.

faq.question: What is the Moot Board of Governors?
faq.answer: The MBG is a national umbrella organization made up of pre-law societies from universities across Canada. Each member society is represented on the Board by its president or a designated representative. Together, the Board coordinates national mooting and advocacy initiatives.

faq.question: How does a society join the Board?
faq.answer: Any recognized undergraduate pre-law or mooting society at a Canadian university may apply to join. Reach out through our contact page and a current Board representative will follow up with the next steps.

faq.question: What is mooting?
faq.answer: Mooting is a simulated court proceeding where participants argue a legal case before a panel of judges. It develops skills in legal research, written advocacy, and oral presentation — a debate structured around a legal dispute with formal court procedure.

faq.question: Who can compete in national competitions?
faq.answer: Students from any member society in good standing are eligible to compete in Board-sanctioned national competitions. Individual competitions may set their own eligibility rules, which are published on each competition's listing.
